claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 21 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 11 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit in the UK.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start:

**Do you have any children you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me:
- How many children you have
- Their names and ages

This will help me understand your situation and guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children. I have three children actually. There's Alex who's 21, Blake who's 15, and Charlie who's 11.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have three children: Alex (21), Blake (15), and Charlie (11).

Let me ask you about each child's situation, starting with some general questions:

**Are Alex, Blake, and Charlie all currently in education or training?** 

Specifically:
- Is Alex (who is 21) still in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?
- Is Blake (who is 15) in education?
- Is Charlie (who is 11) in education?

This is important because Child Benefit eligibility can extend beyond age 16 if a young person is still in qualifying education or training.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Charlie is 11, so yes, he's at school - primary school still.

Blake is 15 and in secondary school, doing GCSEs.

As for Alex, no - Alex is 21 and working full-time now, not in education anymore.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So to summarize:
- Charlie (11) is in primary school ✓
- Blake (15) is in secondary school doing GCSEs ✓
- Alex (21) is working full-time and not in education

Now I need to ask a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Do all three children live with you?** Or does any of them live with someone else, or are any of them in hospital or care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't all live with me. Charlie lives with me at home, but Alex and Blake don't live with me - they live elsewhere.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is important for the assessment.

**Can you tell me more about Alex and Blake's living arrangements?**

Specifically:
- Who do Alex and Blake live with? (For example, do they live with another parent, a relative, or someone else?)
- Even though they don't live with you, do you still contribute to their upbringing or support them financially?

This will help me determine if you might still be eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Alex doesn't live with me - they live with their other parent. But I do help out financially - I contribute about £50 a week towards their upkeep.

As for Blake, they also don't live with me. I do give some money towards Blake's costs, but it's not much - only about £10 a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details. This is helpful information.

Now I need to understand who might already be claiming Child Benefit for these children:

**Is the other parent (or anyone else) already claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

And just to clarify - you mentioned you contribute financially to both Alex and Blake even though they don't live with you. Are you contributing at least the Child Benefit rate (which is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child and £16.95 per week for additional children) towards their upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex, no - the other parent isn't claiming Child Benefit for them. Nobody else is claiming it.

For Blake, yes - their other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

As for the amounts - yes, for Alex I'm paying £50 a week, which is more than that rate you mentioned. For Blake, I'm only contributing £10 a week, so that's less than the Child Benefit rate.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. Let me make sure I understand the full picture for each child:

**Alex (21):** Lives with other parent, no one is claiming Child Benefit, you contribute £50/week
**Blake (15):** Lives elsewhere, other parent IS claiming Child Benefit, you contribute £10/week  
**Charlie (11):** Lives with you

Now, a few final questions:

1. **Are you responsible for Charlie?** (i.e., you're their parent or you're looking after them)

2. **Do you or your partner (if you have one) earn over £50,000 per year?** (This doesn't affect eligibility but may affect whether you need to pay a tax charge)

3. **Are you currently living or working in the UK, or do you have a connection to the UK through residence or work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I'm Charlie's parent and I'm responsible for them - they live at home with me.

I do live in the UK, yes - we're based in Manchester.

As for income, I'd rather not go into all those details right now - I'm just trying to understand if I'm eligible first.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's absolutely fine - I have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit now.

Let me review each child's situation carefully:

**For Charlie (11):** Charlie lives with you, is under 16, is in school, and you are responsible for them. You meet the basic eligibility requirements for Charlie.

**For Blake (15):** Although Blake is under 16 and you contribute financially, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any time. Since someone else is already claiming for Blake, you cannot also claim for them unless that person stops claiming.

**For Alex (21):** Alex is 21 years old and not in education or approved training. Child Benefit can only be claimed for young people aged 16-19 if they are in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 and working full-time, they are over the age limit even for the extended provisions, so you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on this assessment, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie only.**

Let me now record this formal assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary

**You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.**

You are not eligible for Alex or Blake for the reasons explained above.

If you'd like to proceed with claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, you can apply online or by post. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old and working full-time, not in education or approved training. Child Benefit can only be claimed for children under 16, or for young people aged 16-19 who are in approved full-time non-advanced education or approved training. Since Alex is over 20 and not in qualifying education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16) and in secondary school, which would normally make the claimant eligible. However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at any time. Since another person is already claiming for Blake, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake unless the other parent stops their claim.

Charlie is 11 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant, is in primary school, and the claimant is responsible for Charlie as their parent. The claimant meets all the basic eligibility requirements: they are responsible for a child under 16 who lives with them in the UK. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children: Alex (21), Blake (15), and Charlie (11). Only Charlie lives with the claimant. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie only, as Charlie is under 16 and lives with them. They are not eligible for Alex because Alex is over the age limit (21 and not in qualifying education). They are not eligible for Blake because another person is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim per child.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 21 years old and working full-time, not in education or approved training. Child Benefit can only be claimed for children under 16, or for young people aged 16-19 who are in approved full-time non-advanced education or approved training. Since Alex is over 20 and not in qualifying education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16) and in secondary school, which would normally make the claimant eligible. However, Blake's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at any time. Since another person is already claiming for Blake, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake unless the other parent stops their claim.

Charlie is 11 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant, is in primary school, and the claimant is responsible for Charlie as their parent. The claimant meets all the basic eligibility requirements: they are responsible for a child under 16 who lives with them in the UK. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children: Alex (21), Blake (15), and Charlie (11). Only Charlie lives with the claimant. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie only, as Charlie is under 16 and lives with them. They are not eligible for Alex because Alex is over the age limit (21 and not in qualifying education). They are not eligible for Blake because another person is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim per child.
